did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper explicitly uses machine learning algorithms, specifically K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN), Naïve Bayes, and Linear Support Vector Machine (LSVM), to classify dermoscopic images of skin lesions as melanoma or non-melanoma. The study focuses on reducing false negative detection of melanoma, a dermatology task, by applying these computational models to extract features based on the ABCD rule and perform binary classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is a conference proceeding from ICEET 2022 that presents original experimental work. It describes a comparative study using machine learning algorithms (KNN, Naïve Bayes, LSVM) on a specific dataset (Interactive Atlas of Dermoscopy) to classify skin lesions. The authors propose a methodology, implement models, and report new empirical results including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and false negative rates, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Comparative Study of Binary Classifiers for Reducing False Negative Detection of Melanoma in Skin Lesions
journal: 2022 International Conference on Engineering and Emerging Technologies (ICEET)
brief_summary: This study addresses the early diagnosis of melanoma by developing a non-invasive classification system to minimize false negative detection in skin lesions. The researchers utilized dermoscopic images of melanoma lesions less than 0.76mm in thickness from the Interactive Atlas of Dermoscopy dataset. Features were extracted based on the ABCD rule (asymmetry, border, colour, and diameter) and fed into three binary classifiers: K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN), Naïve Bayes, and Linear Support Vector Machine (LSVM). The models were evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and false negative rate metrics. The LSVM algorithm achieved the best performance with a classification accuracy of 85% and a false negative rate of 5%, outperforming KNN and Naïve Bayes which had higher false negative rates.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on classifying dermoscopic images of melanoma lesions less than 0.76mm in thickness from the Interactive Atlas of Dermoscopy dataset. It is the primary positive class in the binary classification task.

The binary classification task distinguishes between melanoma and non-melanoma. The dataset contained 160 images total, implying the remainder were non-melanoma lesions, though specific subtypes of non-melanoma are not detailed.

all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of dermoscopic skin lesion images into two discrete classes: melanoma and non-melanoma. The study compares K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN), Naïve Bayes, and Linear Support Vector Machine (LSVM) classifiers. The primary goal is to minimize false negatives in this binary diagnostic task. Metrics reported include accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and false negative rate.

datasets_used.name: Interactive Atlas of Dermoscopy
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset provided by Simon Fraser University, containing images captured using a Dermaphot/Optotechnik dermoscope at 768 x 512 pixels. The study selected images of melanoma lesions less than 0.76mm in thickness (early stage, in-situ or epidermis only) and non-melanoma cases. Images were required to be complete (entire border visible) and in focus.

use.training: Used to train KNN, Naïve Bayes, and LSVM models. The 120 training images were split via five-fold cross-validation.
use.validation: Five-fold cross-validation was employed on the training set to generate independent datasets and tune parameters (e.g., K for KNN).
use.testing: Used to evaluate the trained models. The 40 test images were divided evenly between melanoma and non-melanoma classes.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for the generation of independent datasets from the training set to tune parameters (e.g., K for KNN) and evaluate model performance.
Used to calculate and report classification metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, false positive rate, and false negative rate for each classifier.
Used in the Naïve Bayes classifier to estimate the mean and standard deviation of the training data for each class to calculate posterior probabilities.
Used in the K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN) classifier to calculate the distance between test and training samples to identify the nearest neighbors.
Used in the Linear Support Vector Machine (LSVM) to allow for miscalculations and avoid overfitting when data is not perfectly separable.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, false negative rate) derived from confusion matrices on a fixed test set.

dataset_details.class_distribution_test: Evenly divided between melanoma and non-melanoma (20 each)

Linear Support Vector Machine (LSVM).notes: Optimal classifier with the lowest false negative rate.

K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN).notes: K value set to 10 provided the lowest false negative rate.

Naïve Bayes.notes: Used Gaussian (normal) distribution for probability estimation.

Interactive Atlas of Dermoscopy.notes: The LSVM algorithm outperformed KNN and Naïve Bayes in terms of sensitivity (95% vs 81%) and false negative rate (5% vs 19%). All classifiers differentiated images with varying degrees of success. The study prioritized minimizing false negatives.

architecture_used.main_model: Three binary classifiers: K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN), Naïve Bayes, and Linear Support Vector Machine (LSVM)
architecture_used.input_size: Features extracted from 768 x 512 pixel dermoscopic images; specifically four features: asymmetry index, border index, colour, and diameter (ABCD rule)

KNN.k_value: 10 (optimized from range 1-15 to minimize false negatives)
KNN.tie_breaking: Class with the nearest neighbor is used

Naive_Bayes.probability_estimation: Mean and standard deviation of training data estimated for each class

training_details.validation_method: Five-fold cross-validation on training set (120 images)

preprocessing_techniques: Median filter applied to remove noise from images
Histogram stretching applied to improve contrast
Image segmentation using morphological processes of opening and closing
Quality control: Images rejected if incomplete (border not visible) or out of focus
conclusions: The Linear Support Vector Machine (LSVM) algorithm was identified as the optimal classifier for reducing false negative detection of melanoma, achieving an accuracy of 85%, sensitivity of 95%, specificity of 74%, and a false negative rate of 5%.
LSVM outperformed K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN) and Naïve Bayes, which both achieved lower accuracy (75% and 73% respectively) and higher false negative rates (19% each).
Minimizing false negatives is critical in melanoma diagnosis to ensure timely treatment, and the proposed LSVM method effectively prioritizes this over false positives.
The study acknowledges limitations regarding the small size and quality of the dataset (160 images), noting that poor capturing techniques led to image exclusion.
The authors suggest that a larger, high-quality dataset would be necessary to enable the use of more complex models such as neural networks.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a very small dataset size (160 images total, with only 120 used for training and 40 for testing), which restricts the generalizability of the results.
The dataset consists exclusively of early-stage melanoma lesions (thickness < 0.76mm) from the Interactive Atlas of Dermoscopy, limiting clinical relevance to advanced or thicker melanomas.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on a held-out test set from the same source dataset.
The paper acknowledges that poor image capturing techniques led to the exclusion of images, introducing potential selection bias regarding image quality.

The study prioritizes minimizing false negatives over false positives, which is clinically appropriate for melanoma screening but results in lower specificity (e.g., 74% for LSVM) and higher false positive rates.
The authors note that the small dataset size prevents the use of more complex models like neural networks, which typically require larger training data.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was reported to validate the differences in performance between the classifiers.
Feature extraction relied on the ABCD rule implemented via a custom GUI, which may introduce subjectivity or variability compared to automated feature extraction methods.
